title: Operation Epic Fury and Its Discontents: A Geopolitical Winners and Losers Assessment
excerpt: A rigorous geopolitical assessment of who gains and who loses from the US-Israel war on Iran — and why the answers are far more counterintuitive than Washington and Tel Aviv would like to admit.
body:
On 28 February 2026, the United States and Israel launched a coordinated series of strikes against Iran under the operational codename Epic Fury — targeting military infrastructure, nuclear sites, and the regime's leadership, including the assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. The operation marked the most significant direct military confrontation between the United States and Iran since the 1979 revolution, and its consequences have reverberated far beyond the theatre of conflict itself.

Wars of this magnitude are rarely contained. They redistribute power, expose vulnerabilities, accelerate existing tensions, and produce winners and losers far removed from the battlefield. Operation Epic Fury is no exception. Within days of the first strikes, the Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed, global energy markets convulsed, allied coalitions fractured, and the strategic calculus of powers from Moscow to Beijing shifted perceptibly.

This analysis does not seek to adjudicate the war's legality or morality. Its purpose is strictly geopolitical: to assess, with the benefit of the first weeks of the conflict, which actors have gained from this rupture in the international order, which have lost, and why. The conclusions are, in several cases, deeply counterintuitive; and in almost every case, they complicate the narratives of victory and defeat that both Washington and Tel Aviv have sought to project.


THE OBJECTIVES OF THE US-ISRAEL COALITION

The war aims of the US-Israel coalition have remained conspicuously undefined. This ambiguity appears, in part, deliberate: by refraining from formally articulating objectives, the Trump administration preserves the capacity to frame any incremental development as a strategic victory. Such constructive vagueness is a recognisable feature of conflicts in which domestic political optics take precedence over operational clarity.

On the ground, the prospect of a conventional land campaign was never a realistic option. Iran's vast territory (1.65 million km²) and complex topography present formidable logistical and military challenges that effectively foreclose any large-scale ground deployment by American or Israeli forces.

If the ultimate objective was the dismantling of the Islamic Revolutionary regime, the premise itself is flawed. Every eliminated commander, from the supreme leader downward, is replaceable within a system designed precisely to absorb such losses. Israel's military establishment understands this dynamic well. History offers a pointed parallel: the Soviet Union did not collapse under external military pressure, but only when its own armed forces declined to defend it. These revolutionary regimes do not fall from the outside; they implode from within. The Soviet collapse, driven by economic exhaustion, ethnic fragmentation, and ultimately the military's refusal to defend the system, stands as a case in point — a point underscored by political scientist Brian D. Taylor, who notes that the army ultimately "chose not to intervene to prevent the collapse of the Soviet Union" (Taylor, Politics and the Russian Army, Cambridge University Press, 2003).


IRAN

Assessing Iran's position in this conflict requires a more nuanced framework than a simple binary of victory and defeat. The Iranian regime sustained significant damage from the strikes — but it was never a regime designed to minimise losses. Built on a doctrine of resistance and ideological endurance, the Islamic Republic has historically demonstrated a capacity to absorb punishment that would destabilise more conventional states. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, in particular, operates as a diffuse, ideologically cohesive structure, and the elimination of individual commanders, however symbolically significant, does not fundamentally alter its operational logic.

More critically, Iran did not remain passive. By closing the Strait of Hormuz, through which a substantial share of global oil supply transits, Tehran succeeded in exporting the cost of the conflict onto the international economy. Simultaneously, its network of allied militias across the region, embedded in countries hosting American military installations, enabled a campaign of sustained pressure on US forward positions. These were not the actions of a losing party; they were the instruments of a state playing, as it always has, on a longer strategic horizon than its adversaries.

This asymmetry of time is perhaps the most persistent blind spot in American strategic culture. History offers a damning pattern: in Vietnam, a technologically inferior adversary outlasted the most powerful military apparatus in the world through sheer ideological resolve and territorial familiarity. In Afghanistan, twenty years of sustained American military presence ended in a withdrawal that restored to power the very forces it had sought to eradicate. In both cases, the United States won battles and lost the war — not on the battlefield, but in the dimension of time. Iran, acutely aware of this precedent, has structured its entire strategic posture around the same principle.

The Trump administration's subsequent decision to lift sanctions on Iranian oil exports only complicated this picture further, providing the regime with an economic reprieve at a moment when sustained pressure might otherwise have compounded its difficulties.


RUSSIA

Russia emerges as one of the principal beneficiaries of the conflict, albeit through means that are characteristically indirect. Rather than engaging militarily, Moscow pursued a strategy of intelligence sharing with Iran, providing satellite imagery of American warship and aircraft positions, and offering specific tactical advice on drone operations — consolidating a strategic partnership while simultaneously observing, in real combat conditions, how Western military systems perform under fire.

The economic dimension is equally significant. As the Financial Times noted in its March 18 editorial, the conflict has generated a substantial financial dividend for Moscow, with Russia's additional revenues from rising oil prices on world markets projected to reach between $3.3 and $4.9 billion by the end of March alone — a direct consequence of a global supply disruption that the United States itself helped trigger. The Trump administration, in seeking to stabilise energy markets, inadvertently handed its principal strategic adversary a considerable fiscal lifeline — one that directly strengthens Russia's capacity to sustain its war effort in Ukraine.

The conflict, in this sense, has functioned for Moscow as both an intelligence dividend and an economic subsidy — costly to others, essentially free to itself.


CHINA

China's position in this conflict is one of structural ambivalence — simultaneously absorbing significant economic damage while accruing considerable geopolitical capital.

As the world's largest importer of Iranian oil, Beijing was immediately and severely exposed to the consequences of the war. Before the conflict, China received 5.35 million barrels of oil per day via the Strait of Hormuz; that figure dropped to roughly 1.22 million once the war began (Foreign Policy, March 2026). The disruption constitutes, by any measure, a major economic shock — compounded by rising global prices and the sudden inaccessibility of discounted Iranian crude that Beijing had relied upon under the terms of its 2021 comprehensive strategic partnership with Tehran.

Geopolitically, however, the calculus is considerably more favourable. The continued erosion of international law and norms surrounding sovereignty and the use of force could lower the political costs of coercive diplomacy in other theatres, including Taiwan. If the conflict extends into a prolonged war, the sustained deployment of major US military assets in the Middle East could divert Washington's attention, pushing China to consolidate its position in the Asian theatre (Middle East Council on Global Affairs, March 2026). The Trump administration's conduct — its unilateral military campaign against Iran, and its seizure of Venezuelan President Maduro — has furnished Beijing with a permissive strategic environment. With the US military depleted and distracted by a conflict on the other side of the globe, observers have warned that Xi Jinping may never have a better opportunity to move on Taiwan (Time, March 2026).


ISRAEL

For Benjamin Netanyahu, the war represents the culmination of a decades-long strategic campaign. Since the assassination of Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin in 1995 — which brought Netanyahu to power for the first time — successive Israeli governments have sought to place Iran's military and nuclear infrastructure at the centre of American foreign policy, framing it as an existential threat requiring direct US involvement. Where diplomacy, sanctions, and covert operations had repeatedly fallen short of that threshold, the Trump administration's willingness to engage militarily marks an unprecedented breakthrough.

The achievement is as much political as it is military. Netanyahu succeeded where many had deemed it impossible: translating a long-standing Israeli strategic objective into American action, at a moment when Washington's own National Security Strategy had formally committed to disengaging from the Middle East — explicitly reframing the region as a space for investment and transactional partnership rather than military intervention (The White House, National Security Strategy, 2025). Whether the military outcomes ultimately justify the regional costs remains an open question — but the diplomatic victory, for Israel, is already secured.


THE UNITED STATES

If the war produced a clear strategic winner in Israel and an ambiguous one in Russia, it produced something closer to diplomatic isolation for the United States. Washington launched Operation Epic Fury without prior consultation with its closest European allies and then expected those same allies to provide bases, political backing, and military support.

The European response drew a firm line between defensive and offensive participation. As Lara Jakes and Catherine Porter reported in the New York Times, Europe mounted a rare show of defensive force — one that simultaneously exposed the limits of its military capacity, diverting already stretched resources from other theatres of concern. German Foreign Minister Wadephul stated explicitly that Berlin had no intention of joining military operations, demanding clarity from Washington and Tel Aviv on their objectives before any discussion of a broader regional security architecture could begin (Al Jazeera, March 2026).

When Trump subsequently called on NATO allies to join a military coalition to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the request was met with near-universal refusal. Trump rebuked his allies publicly, calling their decision a "very foolish mistake" — before declaring that the United States did not need them anyway. It was a remarkable statement from the leader of an alliance built on collective defence, and a measure of just how completely the war had severed Washington from the partners it had spent decades cultivating.


LEBANON

Lebanon stands as perhaps the most acute victim of this conflict — not as a direct belligerent, but as a state whose chronic internal fragmentation renders it structurally incapable of insulating itself from regional upheaval. As L'Orient-Le Jour reported, Israeli strikes on Lebanese territory had, by late March 2026, caused over 1,000 deaths and nearly 2,900 wounded, with more than one million people displaced — figures that place this escalation among the most devastating episodes in Lebanon's recent history.

The country's long-standing confessional divisions, never fully resolved since the civil war, have once again functioned as a transmission mechanism: absorbing the shockwaves of a conflict it did not choose, and translating them into an existential domestic crisis. Hezbollah's decision to enter the war on 2 March — in response to the killing of Supreme Leader Khamenei — sharpened this dynamic considerably, reopening the fundamental question of whether the Lebanese state retains any meaningful sovereignty over its own territory and security decisions.

The result is a country caught, as it has been so many times before, between the competing imperatives of regional powers — unable to remain neutral, and too fragile to survive being drawn in.


GULF COUNTRIES

From the outset, the Gulf states wanted no part of this war. As the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace reported, Gulf state diplomats repeatedly tried to distance their countries from any push for military action, using their unique access to the Trump administration to steer Washington toward a new nuclear deal rather than open conflict — all to no avail.

The consequences have been severe. Iran rapidly moved beyond striking US and Israeli targets and chose instead to externalise the pain of the conflict by hitting civilian infrastructure across all six GCC states — oil platforms, refineries, airports, seaports, hotels, and commercial vessels. The Gulf states, which had spent decades and trillions of dollars cultivating a reputation as oases of stability in an otherwise volatile region, found that reputation shattered within days. As Allison Minor argued in the Atlantic Council, the Iranian assault on the UAE alone — over ten times more intense than the 2022 Houthi attacks — forced a wholesale reassessment of the Gulf's security model.

Dubai stands as the most emblematic victim of this recalibration. For years, the city marketed itself as the capital of global capitalism — a tax haven, a diplomatic crossroads, a place where pragmatic leadership insulated commerce from regional brutality. As Time noted, that carefully constructed image is precisely what Iran targeted: attacks on the Palm Jumeirah, Dubai International Airport, and the Jebel Ali port struck not just infrastructure but the symbolic architecture of Dubai's brand. The region risks some of the most severe economic harm since the 1990–1991 Gulf War, with the Gulf losing hundreds of millions of dollars per day in economic activity across aviation, tourism, energy, and logistics (Al Jazeera, March 2026).


EUROPE AND GREAT BRITAIN

Europe enters this conflict as a secondary victim — not a belligerent, but a continent absorbing damage it neither caused nor consented to. The energy dimension has been particularly brutal. Europe woke to another energy shock as military strikes on infrastructure in Qatar and Iran sent oil and gas prices soaring to levels unseen since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 — with Brent crude surging over $114 per barrel and European natural gas prices jumping by 60% since the start of the conflict (South China Morning Post, 19 March 2026).

The structural irony is considerable. Having spent the years since 2022 painfully weaning itself off Russian energy, Europe now finds that its new dependencies — primarily Qatari LNG and Gulf supply routes transiting the Strait of Hormuz — have proven no less fragile. As Lisa Basquel argued in the Atlantic Council, an obvious release valve exists — reimporting Russian pipeline gas — but it remains politically toxic: reopening that route would hand Moscow a significant revenue stream at precisely the moment when the war in Ukraine remains unresolved (Atlantic Council, 17 March 2026).

Yet that is effectively what the Trump administration has done, albeit indirectly. Washington temporarily lifted sanctions on Russian oil at sea in order to calm global energy markets — a decision met with dismay across Europe, where officials feared it would directly strengthen the Russian war machine (New York Times, 12 March 2026). The move encapsulates Europe's predicament with surgical precision: a war it opposed, fought without its consent, is now forcing it to choose between energy security and the strategic commitments it has made to Ukraine. In both cases, the cost is paid in Europe — and the beneficiary is Moscow.
